title: 2020 Dean's Report
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## January 26, 2020 | Dean's Report Sermon

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Third Sunday after Epiphany in the Christian calendar, falling within the Epiphany season (January 6 - February 8). The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date included 1 Corinthians 12:12-31a (on spiritual gifts and the body of Christ) and Matthew 4:12-23 (Jesus calling his first disciples). The liturgical year was in Ordinary Time, a period focused on Christ's ministry and teachings rather than major feast days.

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the interim period following the 79th General Convention (2018), with no major denominational events scheduled for early 2020. The Diocese of Colorado was under the leadership of Bishop Rob Skirving (consecrated 2013). No significant diocesan crises or major bishop elections were occurring at this moment, though the broader Anglican Communion continued wrestling with issues of LGBTQ+ inclusion and biblical interpretation that had dominated recent conventions.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced typical winter conditions in late January with temperatures in the 30s-40s Fahrenheit and dry weather. Mayor Michael Hancock's administration was managing ongoing affordable housing and homelessness initiatives in the Capitol Hill neighborhood where the Cathedral sits. The Colorado legislature was in early session mode (begun January 8, 2020), with education funding and healthcare access among the priority issues being debated at the State Capitol, just blocks from the Cathedral.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

President Trump's impeachment trial was underway in the Senate, having begun January 16 with opening arguments, dominating national news coverage. The Iowa caucuses were scheduled for February 3 (one week later), with Democratic primary candidates intensely campaigning in Iowa and nationwide. The COVID-19 pandemic was emerging as a concern in China with several cases reported in Wuhan, though it had not yet reached the United States and was not yet part of American news consciousness at this date.

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The WHO held a meeting on January 22-23 regarding the novel coronavirus outbreak in China but did not initially declare a Public Health Emergency of International Concern; the global health threat was not yet widely recognized outside Asia. The US-Iran tensions remained elevated following the January 3 killing of Iranian General Soleimani and Iran's retaliatory missile strikes on January 8, though direct military escalation had paused. Australia was still recovering from unprecedented bushfire season that had peaked in early January, with fires continuing to burn and smoke affecting air quality across the continent.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood was fractious and deeply polarized over the impeachment proceedings, with Americans divided along partisan lines about the President's fitness for office. Super Bowl LIV was scheduled for February 2 (one week away), with the Kansas City Chiefs and San Francisco 49ers generating sports interest. There was a widespread sense of normalcy in American life despite political turbulence; most people were unaware that a pandemic would fundamentally alter society within weeks, making this sermon moment a snapshot of the pre-COVID world.
